Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination
Create a marketing-focused caregiving control system manual for adult children coordinating care for an aging parent.

Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination

Within 90 days of system launch, establish a fully operable caregiver command center with all 8 domains documented, legal and financial authority status verified, a 12-month funding runway mapped, one owner assigned per function, and an emergency packet ready for immediate use so that no major care decision is made without records, authority, and current documentation.

Time Horizon
Initial stabilization in the first 90 days, then ongoing quarterly control reviews for the duration of the parent's care trajectory
Document ID
LSB-00001
Document Guide

Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination 2
System Overview

Operational Dashboard

System Objective
Within 90 days of system launch, establish a fully operable caregiver command center with all 8 domains documented, legal and financial authority status verified, a 12-month funding runway mapped, one owner assigned per function, and an emergency packet ready for immediate use so that no major care decision is made without records, authority, and current documentation.
8
Control Domains
Initial stabilization in the first 90 days, then ongoing quarterly control reviews for the duration of the parent's care trajectory
Time Horizon
35
Worksheets
Milestones
  1. 1
    Caregiver Command Center Established
    Documentation hub, master contacts list, communication protocol, review calendar, and emergency packet are assembled in one accessible system and verified by the primary caregiver.
  2. 2
    Authority Status Verified Across Legal and Medical Decisions
    All existing POAs, directives, releases, wills, trusts, and institutional acceptance status are inventoried, and every missing or invalid authority document is flagged for action.
  3. 3
    Parent Financial Map and 12-Month Funding Runway Completed
    Income sources, accounts, recurring bills, care costs, insurance, and benefit options are documented with a forward-looking funding estimate showing whether care is sustainable for the next 12 months.
  4. 4
    Home and Daily Care Risk Controls Activated
    Home hazards, medication risks, appointment coordination, provider contacts, and contingency care coverage are documented and all urgent safety actions are assigned to owners.
  5. 5
    Family Governance Model Assigned and Logged
    One owner per function is formally assigned, communication rules are documented, and decision logging begins with date-stamped records for major care, spending, and legal actions.
  6. 6
    End-of-Life and Emergency Readiness Completed
    Advance care wishes, emergency escalation contacts, hospice readiness indicators, and immediate-after-death first-step instructions are documented and added to the emergency packet.
Success Criteria
  • All 8 control domains have a current written status, named owner, and documented next action within 90 days of system launch.
  • A command center containing the documentation hub, master contacts, communication protocol, review calendar, and emergency packet is completed and accessible to the primary caregiver and backup coordinator within 30 days.
  • Legal authority status for health decisions, financial decisions, privacy access, and estate documents is verified in writing within 45 days, with every missing or rejected document assigned for follow-up.
  • A 12-month care funding runway showing income, recurring obligations, current care costs, and identified benefit options is completed within 60 days.
  • At least one primary owner and one backup owner are assigned for medication coordination, appointments, financial administration, emergency response, and family communications within 45 days.
  • All major care, legal, and spending decisions are entered into a contemporaneous decision log within 24 hours of the decision date once the system is active.
  • Quarterly cascade reviews across cognitive, legal, financial, care, safety, governance, funding, and end-of-life domains are scheduled for the next 12 months before day 90.
  • The emergency packet includes current medications, diagnoses, provider contacts, insurance information, legal authority documents or status notes, and emergency instructions, and is reviewed at least every 90 days.
System
Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination
Generated
April 12, 2026
Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination 3
01
Section 01

System Architecture

An adult child coordinating an aging parent's cognitive, legal, financial, care, and end-of-life affairs without a documented control system

Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination 8 control domains
System Architecture

Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination

Operating Premise
An adult child coordinating an aging parent's cognitive, legal, financial, care, and end-of-life affairs without a documented control system will be forced into irreversible medical, legal, and spending decisions during a crisis, often before authority, records, funding, and family roles are established.
System Objective
Within 90 days of system launch, establish a fully operable caregiver command center with all 8 domains documented, legal and financial authority status verified, a 12-month funding runway mapped, one owner assigned per function, and an emergency packet ready for immediate use so that no major care decision is made without records, authority, and current documentation.
Control Domains
domain-01

Cognitive Change Detection and Capacity Escalation

Detect, document, and escalate changes in the parent's memory, judgment, orientation, and decision-making capacity before informal family assumptions turn into unsafe care or invalid consent decisions.

In Scope
  • Tracking memory, confusion, wandering, missed medications, unpaid bills, repeated stories, unsafe driving, and judgment changes
  • Recording baseline cognitive observations and date-stamped change logs
  • Identifying triggers for physician evaluation, neuropsychological assessment, or capacity review
  • Linking observed decline to legal authority urgency, supervision level, and safety controls
Outputs: Cognitive change log · Capacity concern escalation checklist · Physician evaluation trigger record · Driving and supervision risk status record
domain-02

Legal Authority Activation for Aging Parent Decision Rights

Ensure the adult child knows exactly what legal authority exists, what is missing, and what must be executed before incapacity or hospitalization blocks access and decision-making.

In Scope
  • Inventorying powers of attorney, health care directives, HIPAA releases, wills, trusts, guardianship status, and beneficiary designations
  • Verifying whether documents are signed, witnessed, notarized, current, and accepted by relevant institutions
  • Identifying where elder-law attorney engagement is required
  • Escalating missing authority when cognitive decline or surgery risk is present
Outputs: Legal authority status matrix · Document location register · Missing-document action list · Institution acceptance verification log
domain-03

Financial Visibility and Spending Control for Parent Care

Create immediate visibility into the parent's income, accounts, bills, obligations, and care spending so the adult child can prevent missed payments, fraud exposure, and uncontrolled care costs.

In Scope
  • Listing bank accounts, retirement income, insurance premiums, recurring bills, debt, subscriptions, and cash flow sources
  • Assigning bill-pay ownership and monitoring overdue or duplicate payments
  • Documenting monthly care costs, household costs, and emergency reserve status
  • Flagging fraud risk, unusual withdrawals, and account access gaps
Outputs: Parent financial account inventory · Monthly care cash-flow worksheet · Recurring bills control register · Fraud and access risk log
domain-04

Benefits Eligibility and Care Funding Runway

Determine how long current resources will fund care and what public or private benefits can legally extend that runway before a care crisis becomes a financial crisis.

In Scope
  • Reviewing Medicare, Medicaid relevance, long-term care insurance, veterans benefits, supplemental insurance, and community program eligibility
  • Estimating out-of-pocket care burn rate and time to funding shortfall
  • Sequencing benefit applications and documentation requirements
  • Tracking referral needs to elder-law, benefits specialists, or placement advisors
Outputs: Benefits eligibility checklist · 12-month care funding runway worksheet · Application requirements tracker · Funding gap escalation plan
domain-05

Home Safety and Living Environment Risk Control

Reduce the immediate risk of falls, medication errors, wandering, fire, neglect, and unsafe living conditions in the parent's home or care setting.

In Scope
  • Assessing entry hazards, bathroom safety, lighting, stairs, kitchen risks, medication storage, firearms, emergency access, and monitoring needs
  • Determining whether the current home remains viable for the parent's cognitive and physical status
  • Documenting urgent modifications, supervision requirements, and relocation triggers
  • Preparing home-based emergency response information
Outputs: Home safety risk assessment · Urgent modification action list · Unsafe-at-home trigger criteria · Emergency access and household information sheet
domain-06

Daily Care Operations and Medical Coordination

Translate the parent's needs into a managed care operating schedule covering appointments, medications, providers, aides, transportation, and contingency coverage.

In Scope
  • Maintaining provider lists, medication lists, appointment calendars, discharge instructions, aide schedules, and transportation plans
  • Assigning daily, weekly, and backup care responsibilities
  • Documenting care routines, symptom changes, and hospital or ER follow-up requirements
  • Escalating breakdowns that require higher-acuity care or different care settings
Outputs: Master care schedule · Medication and appointment tracker · Provider and pharmacy contact sheet · Care handoff protocol · Post-discharge follow-up checklist
domain-07

Family Decision Rights and Caregiver Governance

Prevent sibling conflict, duplicate effort, hidden assumptions, and decision paralysis by documenting who has authority, who must be informed, and how family decisions are recorded.

In Scope
  • Assigning one owner per caregiving function
  • Setting communication rules for updates, emergencies, spending approvals, and care changes
  • Recording family decisions, disagreements, and escalation paths
  • Clarifying role boundaries between local caregivers, remote siblings, spouses, and professional care managers
Outputs: Caregiver role assignment matrix · Family communication protocol · Decision log · Dispute escalation path record
domain-08

Late-Stage Care and End-of-Life Execution Readiness

Ensure the family can execute documented wishes, care transitions, comfort priorities, and immediate after-death tasks without improvising under emotional and medical pressure.

In Scope
  • Confirming advance directives, code status discussions, hospice readiness, funeral preferences, and body disposition instructions
  • Listing who must be called at decline, death, or hospice transition
  • Preparing immediate practical steps for death certificate requests, document access, and household shutdown tasks
  • Coordinating with legal, care, and family governance domains when end-of-life triggers activate
Outputs: End-of-life wishes summary · Hospice and code status readiness checklist · Immediate-after-death action sheet · Priority contact cascade list
Known Failure Modes
  • ✕ The parent loses decision-making capacity before powers of attorney, health directives, or releases are executed, forcing the family into guardianship, delayed treatment consent, or blocked account access.
  • ✕ Cognitive decline is noticed informally but never logged, so legal and safety escalation is delayed until after a fall, wandering event, medication error, or financial exploitation incident.
  • ✕ No single owner is assigned to bill payment and account monitoring, resulting in missed insurance premiums, service shutoffs, overdrafts, or duplicate care spending that destabilizes care within months.
  • ✕ The family begins paying for home care or facility support without calculating burn rate or benefits eligibility, and the parent reaches a funding shortfall before a safe transition plan is available.
  • ✕ Medical, medication, and provider information is scattered across phones, portals, and paper files, causing treatment delays or incorrect decisions during hospitalization or emergency transfer.
  • ✕ Sibling disagreement over who decides and who pays is not governed by a documented communication and authority structure, causing stalled decisions during time-sensitive discharge, placement, or consent events.
  • ✕ Home safety modifications are postponed until after a preventable fall, kitchen incident, wandering event, or failed overnight stay demonstrates that the home is no longer safe.
  • ✕ End-of-life wishes are discussed casually but never documented, so the family faces code status conflict, hospice delay, or unwanted interventions during acute decline.
  • ✕ Review cadence is not maintained, so outdated medication lists, expired contacts, changed finances, or invalid assumptions remain in the system until a crisis exposes them.
  • ✕ Decisions are made without contemporaneous logging, leaving the caregiver unable to reconstruct what was agreed, by whom, and under what authority when disputes or institutional questions arise.
Operating Constraints
⊘ Legal authority is state-specific and may require witnessing, notarization, capacity at signing, and institution-specific acceptance before use.
⊘ The parent's cognitive capacity may narrow the window for valid execution of powers of attorney, directives, and consent-based planning.
⊘ Benefit eligibility and care funding options depend on asset levels, income streams, insurance contract terms, service setting, and application timing.
⊘ Hospitals, banks, insurers, and care facilities may refuse informal family instructions without current, acceptable documentation.
⊘ Family caregivers often operate remotely, after work hours, and across multiple households, reducing response time and increasing handoff risk.
⊘ Care needs can escalate suddenly after falls, hospitalizations, infections, medication changes, or wandering events, compressing planning time from months to hours.
⊘ Documentation becomes operationally unusable if not centralized, dated, and review-maintained; stale records can be as dangerous as missing records.
⊘ Any function without a named owner is likely to fail during a crisis because responsibility defaults to assumption rather than assignment.
⊘ Emergency decisions may need to be made outside normal business hours, requiring a ready packet of legal, medical, and contact information that does not depend on portal access.
⊘ This system must function as a working manual with worksheet-driven outputs across 13 chapters and 50+ worksheets, not as a passive informational guide.
Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination 5
01
Chapter 01

Cognitive Change Detection and Capacity Escalation

Memory Loss, Unsafe Judgment, and Capacity Decline Escalation Control

Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination 4 control domains
Chapter 1 | Cognitive Change Detection and Capacity Escalation

Memory Loss, Unsafe Judgment, and Capacity Decline Escalation Control

You are usually handed this domain in the least efficient way possible: a sibling says your mother is “getting forgetful,” a neighbor mentions she was outside at 2:00 a.m. looking for a dog she has not owned in ten years, a pharmacist flags an early refill pattern, or the parent insists nothing is wrong while the unpaid utility notices are stacked under a fruit bowl. In the Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination, your first job is not to argue with the parent, persuade the family, or diagnose dementia. Your first job is to convert scattered anecdotes into operational evidence that can trigger the next domains before a hospitalist, bank fraud department, Department of Motor Vehicles hearing office, or probate court forces the issue under emergency conditions. This chapter exists to produce four specific outputs: the Parent Cognitive Baseline Observation Table for Memory, Orientation, and Judgment, the Date-Stamped Cognitive Change and Unsafe Incident Log, the Capacity Concern Escalation Checklist for Physician Review and Legal Urgency, and the Unsafe Driving, Wandering, and Medication Self-Management Risk Determination Form. If those outputs are current, the rest of the system can move with speed and legitimacy; if they are missing, every later decision about authority, supervision, driving, home safety, and medical follow-up becomes guesswork disguised as family opinion.

Your operating premise in this domain is that families consistently notice decline long before they can prove it, and institutions consistently demand proof long after the family has become frightened. A primary care physician will not act on “she’s not herself” with the same urgency as three date-stamped incidents of disorientation, missed antihypertensive doses, and a documented instance of getting lost on the route to a church attended for thirty years. An elder-law attorney cannot confidently advise on executing a durable power of attorney or health care directi

…

Domain Purpose
Detect, document, and escalate changes in the parent's memory, judgment, orientation, and decision-making capacity before informal family assumptions turn into unsafe care or invalid consent decisions.
Scope
→ Complete ws-01-a within the first 7 days of launching the caregiver command center using dated, witnessable observations across memory, orientation, judgment, medication handling, bill handling, route-finding, communication coherence, and multistep instruction follow-through — Rationale: a baseline created only after hospitalization, wandering, or financial damage is too weak to prove what changed, which leaves physicians, attorneys, and discharge planners relying on family opinion instead of operational evidence. Trigger: first week of system launch for an aging parent care coordination manual implementation. Risk: delayed physician action, blocked discharge planning, and loss of the legal signing window before Domain-02 can be activated.
→ Write only anchored observations in the cognitive record, such as exact repeated-story loops, wrong month, unpaid bill dates, stove incidents, navigation failures, or duplicate medication events; do not write labels like 'confused' or 'not competent' — Rationale: clinicians, elder-law attorneys, insurers, and courts can act on objective function-linked facts, but vague labels invite resistance and fail to show the specific impairment affecting consent, safety, or finances. Trigger: every entry into ws-01-a or ws-01-b. Risk: physician visits end with 'monitor for now,' sibling disputes intensify, and evidence is too weak to prevent outcomes like a $7,500 guardianship filing or a denied authority decision.
→ If direct discussion with the parent changes ordinary behavior, produces anger, denial, confabulation, or defensive performance, switch immediately to observational logging plus third-party and artifact-based evidence collection — Rationale: once the parent starts masking or disputing, you lose visibility into normal function and contaminate the baseline the rest of the caregiving control system depends on. Trigger: any baseline interview or function review in which the parent becomes unreliable or behavior changes enough to hide ordinary deficits. Risk: the family misses the true pattern until a crisis event forces emergency decisions by a hospitalist, bank fraud department, DMV hearing office, or probate court.
→ Escalate to ws-01-c the same day when a cognitive event has material consequence or repeats twice in 30 days, including stove incidents, missed insulin, traffic confusion, inability to name current medications, wandering, or repeated financial errors — Rationale: families routinely underreact to individual warning signs and never convert aggregate decline into physician review, legal urgency, or supervision change before harm occurs. Trigger: one high-risk incident or 2 similar incidents in 30 days. Risk: unsafe discharge home, invalid document execution, vehicle liability exposure, and weeks of delay while the family reconstructs history after the emergency.
→ Move driving status to formal risk review after any one route-finding failure on a familiar route, confusion at traffic signals, unexplained new vehicle damage, or physician cognitive safety concern; if wrong-way traffic, getting lost, or inability to navigate regular appointments is documented, suspend driving access and secure keys immediately — Rationale: 'still driving locally' hides the exact failure mechanisms that lead to collisions, bodily injury claims, and insurer scrutiny. Trigger: first documented driving red flag in ws-01-d, and immediate suspension for any severe navigation or traffic-orientation failure. Risk: collision exposure, insurer premium spike or nonrenewal, third-party injury liability, and direct family losses such as the $3,600 car replacement described in the narrative.
→ Treat medication self-management as failed when the parent cannot reliably state what each prescription is for, when it is taken, or whether today's dose has already been taken; for insulin, anticoagulants, seizure medications, opioids, heart medications, or more than one prescription category discrepancy, trigger Domain-06 immediately — Rationale: medication breakdown often appears before obvious confusion, and high-risk drug errors create immediate medical danger long before the family recognizes a broader capacity decline. Trigger: any high-risk medication discrepancy, duplicate bottles, early refill irregularity, mismatched pill organizer, or inability to explain current dosing. Risk: duplicate anticoagulant dosing, missed insulin, preventable emergency department use, and unsafe continuation of unsupervised care routines.
→ If the parent is suddenly far more confused, sleepy, agitated, or incoherent over 48 hours after infection, surgery, dehydration, hospitalization, or medication change, treat it as possible acute delirium and seek same-day medical evaluation while documenting the sharp departure from baseline — Rationale: acute delirium is frequently mistaken for 'the dementia getting worse,' which delays treatment for reversible drivers such as urinary tract infection, dehydration, stroke, or medication toxicity. Trigger: abrupt cognitive or behavioral shift over hours to days compared with ws-01-a baseline. Risk: multi-night admission, unsafe first discharge plan, and out-of-pocket costs like the approximately $2,800 described after delayed care.
→ Assign exactly one logging owner for ws-01-b and require all siblings, neighbors, aides, apartment staff, and church contacts to route observations to that owner for standardization into the command center — Rationale: fragmented stories across group texts and family calls prevent anyone from seeing the composite pattern that should trigger legal authority review, supervision change, or physician escalation. Trigger: as soon as more than one observer exists or the first outside report arrives from a sibling, neighbor, pharmacist, aide, or staff member. Risk: late escalation, duplicate or conflicting family decisions, and failure to activate downstream domains before a hospital, bank, or court imposes a timeline.
Worksheets in this Chapter
Parent Cognitive Baseline Observation Table for Memory, Orientation, and Judgment Date-Stamped Cognitive Change and Unsafe Incident Log Capacity Concern Escalation Checklist for Physician Review and Legal Urgency Unsafe Driving, Wandering, and Medication Self-Management Risk Determination Form
In Practice
It is Thursday at 7:12 a.m., and Denise Alvarez is standing in her father's kitchen holding ws-01-d after the pharmacist reports a second early refill on his anticoagulant in 30 days. Because she completed ws-01-a and ws-01-b during the first week of the caregiver command center launch, she can show the primary care office 3 dated incidents: a wrong-month orientation error, a duplicate bill payment, and today's medication discrepancy before the 9:00 a.m. triage cutoff. By 10:40 a.m. the office moves him into a same-day evaluation slot, Denise activates Domain-06 medication supervision, and she removes car keys after matching the refill issue to a recent 'short local trip' where he got lost coming back from church. Without this chapter's logging system, the family would still be arguing over whether he was 'just forgetful' while risking a duplicate anticoagulant dose and another week of unsafe driving.
✓ When This Works
1 Durable power of attorney and health care decision review initiated before hospitalization closes the signing window, avoiding the 5-week delay and approximately $7,500 emergency guardianship cost described in the narrative
2 Same-day escalation of abrupt confusion against a documented baseline reduces delayed-treatment exposure by 6 days and can avoid the approximately $2,800 in out-of-pocket post-ER and recovery costs shown in the acute-change failure scenario
3 Formal driving suspension after the first documented familiar-route failure prevents at least 1 collision event, including the approximately $3,600 vehicle replacement loss and downstream insurer premium spike or nonrenewal risk
4 Thirty-day review cadence converts scattered concerns into a usable physician brief in 1 packet instead of reconstructing months of anecdotes at the appointment, saving an estimated 2-4 weeks of diagnostic delay before neurology, geriatrics, or legal referrals begin
5 Single-owner incident logging produces a complete threshold record with 100% of entries carrying date, observer, behavior, consequence, and domain impact fields, eliminating the undocumented gaps that lead to blocked discharge decisions and unverified family claims
⚠ Cascade Triggers

Downstream systems that activate if this chapter is incomplete or fails.

→ When ws-01-c marks legal urgency active because the parent may still understand agent appointment but the capacity window is narrowing, immediately trigger missing-document review and institution acceptance verification in Legal Authority Activation for Aging Parent Decision Rights
→ When ws-01-c shows the parent cannot understand the role of an agent or follow a basic durable power of attorney explanation, immediately trigger urgent attorney assessment and guardianship/conservatorship pathway review in Legal Authority Activation for Aging Parent Decision Rights
→ When ws-01-d records confirmed wandering, nighttime exit behavior, or inability to state name, address, or emergency contact reliably, immediately trigger supervision review, door-access control, and emergency home-risk response in Home Safety and Living Environment Risk Control
→ When ws-01-d records a stove incident, disorientation at home, or unsafe medication storage tied to memory failure or judgment decline, immediately trigger hazard restriction and home viability review in Home Safety and Living Environment Risk Control
→ When ws-01-c marks physician review required due to changes in orientation, judgment, route-finding, medication adherence, paranoia, hallucination-like confusion, or appointment follow-through failure, immediately trigger appointment scheduling and symptom handoff packet preparation in Daily Care Operations and Medical Coordination
→ When ws-01-d changes driving status to restricted or suspended because of getting lost, traffic-signal confusion, unexplained vehicle damage, or wrong-way travel, immediately trigger transportation substitution planning in Daily Care Operations and Medical Coordination
→ When the cognitive log documents unpaid bills, duplicate payments, suspicious withdrawals, or scam vulnerability linked to decline, immediately trigger account inventory validation and bill-monitoring ownership assignment in Financial Visibility and Spending Control for Parent Care
→ When the command center records disputed observations among siblings or disagreement about whether decline is real, immediately trigger owner confirmation and formal escalation-path enforcement in Family Communication, Roles, and Decision Escalation
Adult Child Caregiver Command System for Aging Parent Care Coordination 7
Chapter 1 · Quick Reference
Cognitive Change Detection and Capacity Escalation

Memory Loss, Unsafe Judgment, and Capacity Decline Escalation Control

Operating Rules
01 Complete ws-01-a within the first 7 days of launching the caregiver command center using dated, witnessable observations across memory, orientation, judgment, medication handling, bill handling, route-finding, communication coherence, and multistep instruction follow-through — Rationale: a baseline created only after hospitalization, wandering, or financial damage is too weak to prove what changed, which leaves physicians, attorneys, and discharge planners relying on family opinion instead of operational evidence. Trigger: first week of system launch for an aging parent care coordination manual implementation. Risk: delayed physician action, blocked discharge planning, and loss of the legal signing window before Domain-02 can be activated.
02 Write only anchored observations in the cognitive record, such as exact repeated-story loops, wrong month, unpaid bill dates, stove incidents, navigation failures, or duplicate medication events; do not write labels like 'confused' or 'not competent' — Rationale: clinicians, elder-law attorneys, insurers, and courts can act on objective function-linked facts, but vague labels invite resistance and fail to show the specific impairment affecting consent, safety, or finances. Trigger: every entry into ws-01-a or ws-01-b. Risk: physician visits end with 'monitor for now,' sibling disputes intensify, and evidence is too weak to prevent outcomes like a $7,500 guardianship filing or a denied authority decision.
03 If direct discussion with the parent changes ordinary behavior, produces anger, denial, confabulation, or defensive performance, switch immediately to observational logging plus third-party and artifact-based evidence collection — Rationale: once the parent starts masking or disputing, you lose visibility into normal function and contaminate the baseline the rest of the caregiving control system depends on. Trigger: any baseline interview or function review in which the parent becomes unreliable or behavior changes enough to hide ordinary deficits. Risk: the family misses the true pattern until a crisis event forces emergency decisions by a hospitalist, bank fraud department, DMV hearing office, or probate court.
04 Escalate to ws-01-c the same day when a cognitive event has material consequence or repeats twice in 30 days, including stove incidents, missed insulin, traffic confusion, inability to name current medications, wandering, or repeated financial errors — Rationale: families routinely underreact to individual warning signs and never convert aggregate decline into physician review, legal urgency, or supervision change before harm occurs. Trigger: one high-risk incident or 2 similar incidents in 30 days. Risk: unsafe discharge home, invalid document execution, vehicle liability exposure, and weeks of delay while the family reconstructs history after the emergency.
05 Move driving status to formal risk review after any one route-finding failure on a familiar route, confusion at traffic signals, unexplained new vehicle damage, or physician cognitive safety concern; if wrong-way traffic, getting lost, or inability to navigate regular appointments is documented, suspend driving access and secure keys immediately — Rationale: 'still driving locally' hides the exact failure mechanisms that lead to collisions, bodily injury claims, and insurer scrutiny. Trigger: first documented driving red flag in ws-01-d, and immediate suspension for any severe navigation or traffic-orientation failure. Risk: collision exposure, insurer premium spike or nonrenewal, third-party injury liability, and direct family losses such as the $3,600 car replacement described in the narrative.
06 Treat medication self-management as failed when the parent cannot reliably state what each prescription is for, when it is taken, or whether today's dose has already been taken; for insulin, anticoagulants, seizure medications, opioids, heart medications, or more than one prescription category discrepancy, trigger Domain-06 immediately — Rationale: medication breakdown often appears before obvious confusion, and high-risk drug errors create immediate medical danger long before the family recognizes a broader capacity decline. Trigger: any high-risk medication discrepancy, duplicate bottles, early refill irregularity, mismatched pill organizer, or inability to explain current dosing. Risk: duplicate anticoagulant dosing, missed insulin, preventable emergency department use, and unsafe continuation of unsupervised care routines.
07 If the parent is suddenly far more confused, sleepy, agitated, or incoherent over 48 hours after infection, surgery, dehydration, hospitalization, or medication change, treat it as possible acute delirium and seek same-day medical evaluation while documenting the sharp departure from baseline — Rationale: acute delirium is frequently mistaken for 'the dementia getting worse,' which delays treatment for reversible drivers such as urinary tract infection, dehydration, stroke, or medication toxicity. Trigger: abrupt cognitive or behavioral shift over hours to days compared with ws-01-a baseline. Risk: multi-night admission, unsafe first discharge plan, and out-of-pocket costs like the approximately $2,800 described after delayed care.
08 Assign exactly one logging owner for ws-01-b and require all siblings, neighbors, aides, apartment staff, and church contacts to route observations to that owner for standardization into the command center — Rationale: fragmented stories across group texts and family calls prevent anyone from seeing the composite pattern that should trigger legal authority review, supervision change, or physician escalation. Trigger: as soon as more than one observer exists or the first outside report arrives from a sibling, neighbor, pharmacist, aide, or staff member. Risk: late escalation, duplicate or conflicting family decisions, and failure to activate downstream domains before a hospital, bank, or court imposes a timeline.
Worksheets
